coredata.dc:title: Bioresource-derived multifunctional carbon quantum dots as a fluorescence and electrochemical sensing platform for picric acid and noncytotoxic food storage application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K.
Thara, Chinnu R.
Korah, Binila K.
John, Neenamol
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: This work deals with the synthesis of facile, water-soluble, and blue-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Plumbago indica leaves by a hydrothermal method with a quantum yield of 16 %. The biomass Plumbago indica leaves exhibit several advances such as easy availability, cost-effectiveness, non-toxic, green source, and not requiring any sophisticated techniques to develop the sensor. The optical, morphological, structural, and compositional properties of the CQDs were confirmed by various characterization methods. As a fluorescent probe, CQDs exhibited good linear response to picric acid (PA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 18 nM. The sensing mechanism involves a combination of static quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the electrochemical sensing method, the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) technique was performed using a CQD-modified gold electrode with a LOD of 0.35 nM for PA. Using fresh carrots as a model sample, the antioxidant activity of PVA, CQD, and a PVA-CQD mixture was qualitatively determined. The in vitro cytotoxicity analysis of CQDs by MTT assay using the L6 cell line confirmed its non-toxic behaviour. This work is noteworthy because it offers a new attempt to identify toxic PA from biomass-derived precursor material and finds future applications in the food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence sensing
Electrochemical sensing
Picric acid
Antioxidant

Determination of picric acid in industrial sewage water
Antioxidant activity of CQD modified PVA film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis. absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of CQDs, insets show the colour of CQD solution in visible light and UV light, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at various excitation wavelengths from 280 to 400 nm.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman analysis of CQDs.
(a)TEM, (b) HR-TEM, (c) SAED, and (d) histogram of the size distribution diagram of CQDs.
XPS spectrum of CQDs; (a) survey spectrum, and high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, and (c) O 1s.

Fluorescence analysis CQDs (a) with various analytes, (b) on increasing the concentration of picric acid from 0 to 5 μM, (c) fluorescence response (F0/F) plot of a mixture of CQDs and picric acid in the concentration range 0 to 0.7 μM, (d) zeta potential diagram of CQDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission response (F/F0) of CQDs-PA mixture with 15 μM interrupting species, and (b) fluorescence lifespan of CQDs before and after adding PA.
(a) CV runs of bare GE, GE/CQD, and GE/CQD/PA, (b) optimization of pH, (c) effect of scan rate, (e) DPV curves, and (f) plot of current vs concentration of PA.
(a) Amperometric curve, (b) repeatability study, (c) stability analysis, and (d) impedance spectra of GE and GE/CQDs.
Plausible mechanism for the detection of PA molecules on the surface of CQD modified GE.
Digital images of untreated carrot, carrot coated with PVA, CQD and CQD-PVA mixture at room temperature.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CQDs.
A comparison between the current work and a few other recent publications.
A comparison between the present work and the previous similar works.
Identification of picric acid in real samples by fluorescence method (n = 3).
Identification of picric acid in real samples by DPV method (n = 3).
The cell viability obtained after incubating L6 cells with CQDs.

head.ce:title: Bioresource-derived multifunctional carbon quantum dots as a fluorescence and electrochemical sensing platform for picric acid and noncytotoxic food storage application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work deals with the synthesis of facile, water-soluble, and blue-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Plumbago indica leaves by a hydrothermal method with a quantum yield of 16 %. The biomass Plumbago indica leaves exhibit several advances such as easy availability, cost-effectiveness, non-toxic, green source, and not requiring any sophisticated techniques to develop the sensor. The optical, morphological, structural, and compositional properties of the CQDs were confirmed by various characterization methods. As a fluorescent probe, CQDs exhibited good linear response to picric acid (PA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 18 nM. The sensing mechanism involves a combination of static quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the electrochemical sensing method, the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) technique was performed using a CQD-modified gold electrode with a LOD of 0.35 nM for PA. Using fresh carrots as a model sample, the antioxidant activity of PVA, CQD, and a PVA-CQD mixture was qualitatively determined. The in vitro cytotoxicity analysis of CQDs by MTT assay using the L6 cell line confirmed its non-toxic behaviour. This work is noteworthy because it offers a new attempt to identify toxic PA from biomass-derived precursor material and finds future applications in the food industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic explosives are widely recognized as hazardous materials since they threaten human health, the environment, and public safety [1]. Picric acid (PA), also known as 2,4,6 trinitro phenol (TNP) is a widely employed chemical reagent and hazardous nitrophenol compound, widely utilized in explosives, organic synthesis, insecticides, dye industries, etc. [2]. A small amount of PA can cause severe contamination of the air, soil, and water sources due to its limited biodegradability, excellent water solubility, explosivity, toxicity, and extremely high acidic behaviour. Even minimal exposure to PA can result in adverse health effects, including but not limited to dizziness, nausea, damage to the skin and eyes, cancer, and respiratory problems [3]. According to the “surface water environmental quality guidelines,” the maximum allowed concentration of PA in water must be lower than 0.5 mg/L [4]. Consequently, the development of selective and efficient sensors for the trace analysis of PA in an aqueous solution with a low detection limit is of great importance. There are several analytical techniques and approaches that have been proposed for the detection of PA, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [6], electrochemistry [7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [8], solid-phase microextraction (SPME) [9], and X-ray imaging techniques [10]. Unfortunately, these analytical procedures are not always feasible due to their high cost, time duration, need for elaborate equipment, and complicated preparation processes.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional multispecialty nanomaterials that can be synthesized through several inexpensive and eco-friendly processing routes. Some of these methods include laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical, microwave, and hydrothermal synthesis [11,12]. Among these, the hydrothermal route has garnered much attention due to its simplicity, eco-friendliness, lack of need for toxic chemicals, and relatively simple procedure [13,14]. CQDs exhibit several interesting properties, such as chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence, ease of surface functionalization, eco-friendly synthesis, and excellent biocompatibility. These inspiring properties make CQDs a potential candidate in different fields, including sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, antioxidants, forensics studies, electronics, and catalysis [15–17].

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a synthetic polymer with excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, high water solubility, low toxicity, and non-carcinogenic properties. PVA can be easily combined with different materials and is widely used in numerous applications such as polymer recycling, preservation of fruits and vegetables, controlled drug delivery systems, and food packaging [18]. CQDs, in general, have excellent antioxidant properties due to the oxygen-containing functionals on their surfaces [19,20]. Several works have been reported on the PVA modified with CQDs; however, only very few discuss their food storage ability. Zhao et al. reported the creation of CQD-PVA food packaging films, which were found to be successful in extending the shelf life of fried meatballs, bananas, and jujubes [21]. In the literature, Eskalen et al. developed PVA-CQD composite using CQDs derived from Rosemary leaves and utilized for food storage applications in bananas as a function of days [22]. The novelty of this work involves the potential of CQD-PVA composite to improve the shelf life of fresh carrots. This method is simple, economical, and CQD was derived from green precursor materials.

This work focuses on the synthesis of unique and multifaceted CQDs from Plumbago indica leaves by the conventional hydrothermal method for the first time. We have selected the biomass Plumbago indica leaves due to the following aspects and has several advantages over other works:(i) It is a commonly available, cost-effective, and non-toxic green source (ii) no sophisticated techniques are needed in the development of the sensor, and (iii) the Plumbago indica plant contains nine compounds, that have a substantial effect on the medicinal and antioxidant properties of the developed CQDs. The CQDs exhibit high quantum yield (QY), minimal toxicity, excellent stability, bright photoluminescence, and desirable electrochemical behaviour. The designed CQDs were developed as an efficient fluorescent and electrochemical probe for the detection of toxic PA molecules. The limit of detection and quantum yield (QY) of the proposed methods is best among several similar reported works. We also investigated the in-vitro cytotoxicity of the synthesized CQDs following the MTT assays using the L6 cell line (rat skeletal muscle cell line). Additionally, by examining the antioxidant characteristics of the PVA-CQD mixture, this research offers a novel strategy that may extend the shelf life of carrots and find significant applications in the food industry. Hence, we have successfully investigated four distinct applications with a single CQD, including fluorescence and electrochemical sensing, for the storage of vegetables and no-cytotoxic studies.

Plumbago indica leaves were obtained from the university campus, phosphate buffer solutions (PBS), quinine sulfate (99.0%), acephate, 2,4,5-trichloronitrobenzene, thiram, phenol, bisphenol, ziram, resorcinol, aniline, thiourea, 4-chlorobenzyl chloride, lindane, 2,4-dichloro phenoxy acetic acid, diuron, sodium phosphate monobasic, sodium phosphate dibasic, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, potassium chloride (KCl), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), of analytical grade were purchased from Merck, India and used without purification. Throughout the experimental procedure, distilled water was utilized.

Fresh Plumbago indica leaves (5 g) were blended with 100 mL of distilled water and placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave during the synthesis procedure. The hydrothermal strategy was applied for the carbonization process by heating the mixture at 200 °C for six hours. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The blue fluorescent CQDs were freeze-dried, dispersed in distilled water to a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL, and stored in the refrigerator. Scheme 1 depicts an overview of the different stages involved in the synthesis process.

For the fluorescence analysis, various concentrations of PA were added to 100 μL of 0.2 mg/mL CQD suspension and 2.5 mL of PBS at pH 6. All the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm at room temperature. The symbols F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of picric acid, respectively. The selectivity of the developed sensor was determined by incorporating different interfering molecules having a 15 μM concentration into a 5 μM CQD-PA solution.

Using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a quantum yield (QY) of 54% as a standard, the QY of the CQDs was determined using the integrated fluorescence analysis. All the analysis was conducted at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm with an absorbance limit of less than 0.1. The quantum yield was evaluated using the equation,
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
μ s
μ R
×
η s
η R
2
where “ΦS” and “ΦR” refers to the QY of the sample and the reference, “μs and μR” represents the slope obtained from the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance plot for the sample and reference, respectively, “ηR and ηs” are the refractive indices of solutions of sample and reference, respectively.

A gold electrode (GE) was chosen as the working electrode in electrochemical experiments, with a platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The CQD suspension was applied to the GE surface and allowed to dry. The cyclic voltammograms (CV) of unmodified GE, CQD-modified GE (GE/CQD), and CQD-modified GE in the presence of various analytes were all examined within the potential ranging from −0.2 to 0.3 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. To examine the reversibility of the sensor, the cyclic voltametric analysis was performed by changing the scan rate from 10 to 90 mV/s. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was applied to various concentrations (5–30 nM) of picric acid to determine the limit of detection (LOD) and linear range of the method. Additionally, the influence of scan rate, the influence of pH, and impedance spectral analysis was conducted to improve the accuracy of the method. The amperometry technique was used to evaluate the selectivity of the developed electrode.

The antioxidant capacity of the PVA-CQD mixture was carried out using the procedures already reported [22]. About 10 g of PVA was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water, followed by heating the mixture to 90 °C with magnetic stirring. Later, about 10 mL of 0.2 mg/mL CQD suspension was added and stirred for 15 min. more. Using a brush, the surface of the carrot was coated with the obtained PVA-CQD solution. The changes that happened to the uncoated, CQD-coated, PVA-coated and PVA-CQD mixture-coated carrots were analyzed over the course of twelve days at room temperature.

L6 cells were seeded on 96 well plates and allowed to acclimatize to the culture conditions of 37 °C and 5 % CO2 environment. The samples were prepared in DMEM media and 0.2 µm Millipore syringe filter was used for the filter sterilization. Five different concentrations of samples including 6.25, 12.5, 25,50, and 100 µg/mL was selected for the analysis. Untreated wells were selected as the control. After incubating the samples for 24 h, the media from the wells were aspirated and discarded. 100 µL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution in PBS was added to the wells. The plates were further incubated for 2 h for the development of formazan crystals. The supernatant was removed, and 100 μL DMSO (100%) were added per well. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured using a microplate reader. Two wells per plate without cells was selected as blank. All the experiments were done in triplicate. The cell viability was calculated using the formula,
(2)
P e r c e n t a g e
o f
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y =
A v e r a g e
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
t r e a t e d
A v e r a g e
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
× 100

UV–vis. absorption and photoluminescence investigations were used to investigate the optical behavior of the CQDs. The absorption peak ( Fig. 1
a) at 280 nm was attributed to n-π* transitions in the CO group and π-π* transition in the aromatic sp2 domains CC and CO bonds [23]. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs display immensely intense emissions over a range of excitation wavelengths from 280 to 400 nm with the most intense emission peaking at 438 nm at an excitation of 330 nm ( Fig. 1 b). The emission wavelength shows a red shift at higher excitation wavelengths, suggesting excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. This may have been caused by the excitation-dependent properties due to the availability of CQDs with a range of sizes, different emissive traps, the existence of oxygen-rich groups, and surface flaws [24]. The produced CQDs exhibit a brown colour when exposed to visible light and a blue fluorescence when excited by UV light (inset Fig. 1 a). The estimated QY of the CQDs was 16 % in comparison to the standard quinine sulphate.

The FT-IR analysis of the developed CQDs ( Fig. 2
a) exhibited a broad vibrational band at 3260 cm−1 due to the OH stretching vibrations. The presence of CH stretching vibrations is suggested by the band at 2935 cm−1. The band at 1620 cm−1 emerged from the symmetric stretching vibrations of CC and CO groups. Bands at 1398 and 1036 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric stretching and symmetric bending vibrations of the CO group, respectively [25]. Due to the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized graphitic carbon atoms, the XRD analysis data displayed a broad distinctive peak at 23.52° corresponding to the (002) plane of CQDs ( Fig. 2 b) [26]. Further studies on the morphology of CQDs was accomplished by Raman spectral analysis ( Fig. 2 c). The Raman analysis of CQDs produced two distinctive bands centered at 1372 (D band) and 1555 cm−1 (G band), caused by the carbonyl groups on the CQD surface and a typical nanostructure core carbon vibration, respectively [27].

To investigate the chemical states and elemental composition of the generated CQDs, XPS analysis was performed. The XPS survey spectrum displays two significant peaks at 285.6 and 530.9 eV, which correlate to the existence of C1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 4
a). The high-resolution analysis deconvoluted the C1s peak at 284.0 eV (Fig. 4 b) into four peaks located at 284.3, 285.1, 285.9, and 286.8 eV, which correspond to the CC, CC, CO, and CO moieties, respectively. The O 1s peak at 590.9 eV was deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks at 532.2, 532.8 and 533.3 eV ( Fig. 4 c), corresponding to the CO, OH and C-O bonds. All of these analyses corroborate the presence of OH, COOH, and -CO functional groups on the surface of the quantum dots.

To investigate the feasibility of CQDs, stability experiments were carried out under varying conditions of ionic strength, pH, UV light exposure, and storage period. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs is not significantly affected by varying the ionic strength using KCl ( Fig. S2 a). The fluorescence intensity was found to be remarkably stable at all pH levels ranging from 3-12 (Fig. S2 b). There was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs after they were exposed to UV radiation for 120 min ( Fig. S2 c). After storing the CQDs at 4 °C for three months, the recorded fluorescence spectra at regular intervals demonstrate their outstanding photostability by displaying no variation in the fluorescence intensities (Fig. S2 d).

Reaction parameters such as pH and incubation time were tuned to get a superior fluorescence sensitivity towards picric acid. CQDs were able to identify picric acid molecules at all tested pH levels, however, the highest level of detection was observed at pH 6 ( Fig. S3 a). To maximize the interaction between CQDs and PA, the reaction time was also optimized. After one minute of incubation, no discernible changes in the fluorescence intensity were observed ( Fig. S3 b). Fluorescence spectroscopic studies were executed to assess the detection ability of the sensor to picric acid by recording the emission spectrum at 330 nm excitation. The sensitivity studies were conducted by introducing several analyte solutions at a concentration of 5 μM to a CQD suspension at pH 6. From the relative fluorescence response (F/F0) versus various analytes plot ( Fig. 5
a) high level of quenching in fluorescence intensity was noticed solely from the PA molecules, while all other analytes failed to interact with the CQDs efficiently. According to concentration studies, increasing the concentration of PA from 0 to 5 μM resulted in a steady decrease in fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 5 b). The linear range and LOD of the detection method was evaluated using the Stern-Volmer equation,
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 + K s v
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of pure CQDs and the CQD-PA mixture at 438 nm, respectively. [Q] denotes the concentration of PA, and Ksv represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. From the Fo/F against concentration graph, a linear graph in the 0––0.7 μM range with a LOD of 18 nM was obtained ( Fig. 5 c). The surface of the CQDs is negatively charged (-25.67 mV) based on the results from the zeta potential examination ( Fig. 5 d). This is because negatively charged groups such as –OH and –COOH are readily available on the CQD surface. The selectivity experiments were performed by mixing 5 μM PA with 15 μM of various interfering compounds ( Fig. 6
a) and the fluorescence results demonstrate that the proposed fluorescent sensor recognized PA molecules selectively even in the presence of other competing chemicals. A comparison of the LOD and linear range of this work with some of the recently reported studies are summarized in Table 1 .

Further comparisons of the optical characteristics of CQDs and PA were performed in order to comprehend the possible mechanisms of fluorescence quenching. The excitation and emission spectra of CQDs and the absorption spectra of PA show significant overlap in the 200–500 nm range ( Fig. S4 ). This shows that the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may be responsible for the reduction in the fluorescence emission. The fluorescence lifetime experiment was executed to determine the lifetime of the CQDs in the absence or presence of PA to further confirming the mechanism. As evident from Fig. 6 b, the fluorescence decay time only slightly changed before and after the addition of PA, going from 4.17 to 4.11 ns, which was consistent with the literature reports [32]. In contrast to FRET, two species in the IFE process should not interact chemically, and the quencher does not affect the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore [33]. Hence, IFE rather than FRET could be the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

In addition, fluorescence quenching can be accomplished by either the static quenching effect or the dynamic quenching effect. In the case of static quenching, fluorescence is quenched by the formation of a nonfluorescent complex between a ground-state fluorophore and the quencher without significantly affecting the fluorescence lifetime. In dynamic quenching, the excited-state fluorophore interacts with the quencher and can be non-radiatively deactivated upon collision, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence lifetime. As previously discussed, the lifetime of the CQDs remains nearly unchanged with the addition of 5 μM PA, indicating that the fluorescence quenching occurs through a static mechanism. In conclusion, a combination of IFE and the static quenching effect are the main mechanisms involved in the CQD-based detection of PA in this work.

The electrochemical behaviour of the sensor was analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a CQD-modified gold electrode (GE/CQD) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The CV curve of the GE/CQD showed an enhanced peak current when scanned in the potential range of −0.2–0.3 V compared to bare GE, confirming the electrochemical activity of the sensor. The introduction of 20 nM picric acid into the detection medium generated intense redox peaks at 0.001 V and −0.11 V (Fig. 7
a). To maximize detection efficiency, the pH of sensing was optimized by measuring the CV curve in PBS over the pH range of 4 to 8, and the maximum current response was measured when the pH of the medium was 6 (Fig. 7 b). Hence, all subsequent analyses were performed at pH 6. The influence of scan rate on the kinetic parameters and mechanism was investigated by means of scan rate studies. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 c, a linear response was observed as the scan rate increased from 10 to 90 mV/s. The effect of concentration on the sensing capability of the modified electrode was explored using DPV analysis in the range of 5–30 nM (Fig. 7 d). The current response increases linearly with the concentration of PA acid in the reaction medium, and a plot of the current value against the concentration of PA results in a linear graph as shown in Fig. 7 e. The LOD was determined using the equation,
(4)
LOD =
3 σ
m
The obtained LOD was 0.35 nM within the linear range of 5–30 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometry, Reproducibility, Stability, and impedance studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Interference studies were performed using the amperometry method to examine the specificity of the sensor for PA molecules. The amperometric curve recorded by mixing 60 nM interfering molecules with 20 nM PA solutions at a pH of 6, produced signals selectively for PA even in the presence of other analyte molecules (Fig. 8
a). There are only a few works has been published on the electrochemical sensing of PA using CQD modified electrodes and a comparison of those with the current study is shown in Table 2 . A continuous run of 70 CV cycles using the GE/CQD in 20 nM PA displayed identical CV curves with the same peak areas and positions ( Fig. 8 b). This accounts for the remarkable reusability of the sensor over a long period. Additionally, the modified electrode was found to be stable even after one month of storage in a desiccator ( Fig. 8 c). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used to measure the resistance of the bare GE and GE/CQD. When GE is modified by CQDs, the resistance significantly decreases, enhancing the conductivity of the modified GE/CQD ( Fig. 8 d).

When PA is introduced onto the surface of a GE/CQD electrode, electron donor hydroxy (–OH) and electron-withdrawing nitro (–NO2) groups are adsorbed on the modified electrode, and a series of redox reactions occur on the modified electrode. Electrons from the GCE/CQDs are responsible for reducing the three NO2 groups to intermediate hydroxyl amino (–HNO-H) groups. These groups undergo further oxidation, which results in the formation of nitroso (N-O) groups. Reversible reduction of the N-O groups results in the release of electrons back into the modified electrode, leading to improved conductivity and current responsiveness [37]. A diagrammatic illustration of the possible mechanism of the electrochemical sensing is given in Fig. 9 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of picric acid in industrial sewage water
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence and an electrochemical method were used for the detection of picric acid in the collected industrial wastewater. The collected samples were initially centrifuged and filtered over a 0.45 μM nylon 6,6 membrane. Different concentrations of picric acid were spiked into the solution and tested for PA by following the proposed method. The experimentally determined recovery values were in the acceptable range (98–101%), with the RSD value less than 2% in all trials, as given in Tables 3
& Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of CQD modified PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The carrot storage experiment was carried out for the qualitative examination of the biological activities of the CQDs incorporated PVA. In this experiment, four carrots were used: one untreated carrot, one coated with PVA, one coated with CQDs, and one coated with a mixture of CQDs and PVA. All the carrots were fresh, with an orange color as shown in Fig. 10 . Photographs were collected at intervals of zero, four, and seven days to track the changes in their appearances, mould formation, and water content. The untreated carrots were severely oxidized after seven days, and a dense layer of mould formation was observed. After 7 days of study, the surface of both the PVA-coated and CQD-coated carrots became rough, water content was reduced, and a layer of fungal infection was observed. However, after 7 days of study, CQDs-PVA mixture-coated carrot surfaces appear almost fresh, with high water content and no mould formation was observed. These findings demonstrated the superiority of the CQD-PVA film as a coating and packaging material for food products. Its capacity to prevent mould development, potential spoiling and moisture loss during storage extends the shelf life of perishable foods, vegetables, and fruits. This substance may hold significant promise for developing more affordable and environmentally friendly ways to control food deterioration.

The MTT assay method was followed to measure the cellular metabolic activity of developed CQDs using the L6 cell line (rat skeletal muscle cell line) cultured in Dulbecco’s modified eagles medium as an indicator of cell viability, proliferation and cytotoxicity. All the experiments were done in triplicate and average values were taken in order to minimize errors.

The cell viability obtained after incubating L6 cells with CQDs at various concentrations (0, 6.5, 12.5, 25, 20, and 100 µg/mL) for 24 h is shown in Table 5 . The percentage cell viability findings are reported in Fig. S5 , indicating that the synthesized CQDs have very low cytotoxicity on L6 cells even at high concentrations (100 µg/mL). The determined LD50 value is 1.6 mg/mL. The greater the LD50, the greater the amount of extract required to produce a toxic response, whereas low LD50 values are extremely toxic and may be hazardous to human health [38, 39]. As a result of the cytotoxicity tests, the CQDs were found to be noncytotoxic, biocompatible, and did not significantly reduce cell viability against L6 cells.

In the current study, a simple, and inexpensive method was developed to fabricate CQD from Plumbago indica leaves and demonstrated four diverse CQD-based applications, including fluorescence sensing, electrochemical sensing, food preservation, and cytotoxic investigations. The CQDs exhibit blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 16 %. The CQDs are water soluble, pH-independent, with excellent shelf life, and remain stable even at high ionic strengths. As a fluorescence sensor for picric acid, CQDs displayed a limit of detection of 18 nM within the linear range of 0–0.7 μM. The remarkable selectivity of CQDs toward PA originates from the combination of FRET and static quenching mechanisms. Selective and rapid electrochemical detection of PA at a pH of 6 was achieved by using a CQD-modified gold electrode (GE/CQD) with a good linear dependence between different concentrations of PA and current value. The linear range was established at a concentration of 5–35 nM with a limit of detection of 0.35 nM. In the analysis of PA in real water samples, the CQD-assisted fluorescence and electrochemical methods demonstrated excellent detection ability, feasibility and repeatability.The potential antioxidant ability of the PVA-CQD mixture was investigated by coating the mixture on fresh carrots and found that PVA-CQD coating improved the shelf life of food items by reducing moisture loss and the fungal growth at room temperature. These findings offer the possible use of fluorescent CQDs as a suitable antioxidant for the production of packages used to wrap perishable fruits and vegetables. Results from the cytotoxicity examination using the MTT assay method showed that the cell viability was greater than 97% after 48 hours of treatment of L6 cell line with CQDs having concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL. These results supported the non-toxic and bio-compatible nature of CQDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from UGC India in the form of Senior Research Fellowship (Grant Number BININ01764209).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2023.06.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
